title: Fed Holds at 3.50–3.75% but Dot Plot Signals Potential 2026 Rate Hike
content: The FOMC voted unanimously on 17 June to hold the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75% in Chair Kevin Warsh's inaugural meeting. The hawkish signal came via the updated dot plot: the 2026 median rose to 3.8%, with nine of 18 officials projecting rates above the current range by year-end, reversing prior easing guidance entirely. The Fed lifted its 2026 headline PCE forecast to 3.6% and core to 3.3%, reflecting US CPI at 4.2% YoY in May — its highest since April 2023. GDP growth was trimmed modestly to 2.2%. UST yields extended their climb and the DXY firmed as markets priced out cuts and began discounting a potential hike later in 2026. Warsh also announced task forces to overhaul Fed communications and operations.

title: Brent Crude Drops ~8% on Week as Israel-Hezbollah Ceasefire Calms Middle East
content: Brent crude fell roughly 8% over the week of 16–20 June, settling near $80/bbl on Friday, as an Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and recovering Strait of Hormuz shipping traffic unwound the war-risk premium built since the Iran conflict erupted in late February. WTI closed near $77/bbl, down ~10% on the week. The move carries significant inflation implications: surging energy costs had pushed US CPI to 4.2% and prompted the ECB's first rate hike since 2023. Formal US–Iran talks in Bürgenstock were abruptly postponed on 19 June, tempering further downside and leaving markets alert to re-escalation risk. Energy equities underperformed, whilst relief on energy-driven inflation expectations provided modest support to global fixed income across the curve.

title: ECB Raises Deposit Rate to 2.25% — First Hike Since 2023 Amid Stagflation Risk
content: The ECB Governing Council raised its deposit rate by 25bp to 2.25% on 11 June — its first hike since September 2023 — as the Iran war drove eurozone HICP to 3.2% YoY in May, the highest reading since September 2023. The new rate took effect on 17 June. Staff projections see headline inflation averaging 3.0% in 2026 and 2.3% in 2027, whilst GDP growth was trimmed to just 0.8% for 2026, raising stagflation concerns across the bloc. The ECB characterised the decision as robust across a range of geopolitical scenarios. EUR/USD firmed initially; Bund yields rose across the curve with 10-year spreads to USTs narrowing. Markets are pricing the possibility of further tightening should energy costs remain elevated.
